Reverse-engineer the ARCHITECTURE of: **dalpha-deepresearch (53.10, rank 5)**

THE LEADERBOARD ON OUR EVALUATOR (GPT-5.5) — this is the ONLY board that matters to us:
  cellcog-max 55.78 | WhaleCloud-DocChain_0612 54.78 | bodhi 54.07 | lunon 53.51 | dalpha-deepresearch 53.10
  sourcery 51.17 | gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.98 (== the "reference", so parity = 0.50) | openai-deepresearch 47.84
  POLARIS (us) 43.82 | perplexity-Research 43.05 | grok-deeper-search 41.22
NOTE: the widely-cited "ADORE 52.65" is on NEITHER board — treat it as unverified. The legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro
board (qianfan 58.03, ZTE 57.27, ...) is a DIFFERENT evaluator and its numbers are NOT comparable to ours.
We already hold 100-task artifacts for: cellcog, tavily, onyx, deepinsight, baidu-qianfan (in /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus).

DO BOTH of these:
1. THE PUBLISHED RECORD. Search hard: arXiv, GitHub, company blog, HF model/dataset card, tech report, WeChat/Zhihu
   posts (several of these are Chinese teams — search Chinese-language sources too: 深度研究, 智能体, 报告生成).
   Is there a paper? Code? A tech report? If there is genuinely NOTHING public, SAY SO — do not invent an architecture.
2. THE ARTIFACT. If we have its reports in /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus (or the Acquire phase just fetched them), READ THEM and measure
   them across MANY tasks, not just task 72. Reverse-engineer the generation process from the text: section/subsection
   structure, paragraph length distribution, whether it uses bullets/tables (and whether that varies BY TASK GENRE),
   how it cites, whether it names studies in prose, how it handles conflicting evidence, whether it has a dedicated
   synthesis/tensions section, whether the writing looks single-pass or multi-step, whether there is evidence of a
   revision/critique pass.

THE QUESTION THAT MATTERS MOST: **how does it COMPOSE the report, and how does it produce SYNTHESIS?**
We are an extraction-grounded fact conveyor — we span-verify every sentence, which forbids cross-source inference.
We score insight 0.4238 (weight 0.32, our worst). Find out what these systems do INSTEAD.

Prefer primary sources. Mark anything unverified. We were burned tonight by a confidently-quoted stale number.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about DeepResearch Bench:

## Overview

DeepResearch Bench is a benchmark consisting of 100 PhD-level research tasks, each meticulously crafted by domain experts across 22 distinct fields.

## Purpose

Deep Research Agents are a prominent category of LLM-based agents that autonomously orchestrate multistep web exploration, targeted retrieval, and higher-order synthesis to transform vast amounts of online information into analyst-grade, citation-rich reports. However, a comprehensive benchmark for systematically evaluating the capabilities of these agents was absent, which is why DeepResearch Bench was created.

## Dataset Composition

The benchmark consists of 100 PhD-level research tasks--50 in Chinese and 50 in English--spanning 22 distinct fields.

## Evaluation Framework

Two complementary evaluation frameworks assess different aspects of DRA capabilities: RACE (Reference-based Adaptive Criteria-driven Evaluation framework with Dynamic Weighting) for evaluating the quality of generated research reports, and FACT (Framework for Factual Abundance and Citation Trustworthiness) for assessing information retrieval effectiveness and citation accuracy.

## Availability

DeepResearch Bench and key components of the frameworks have been open-sourced at https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench to accelerate the development of practical LLM-based agents.

## Dalpha AI

Dalpha is an artificial intelligence agent startup that ranked first in several major global AI benchmarks, outperforming large technology companies. The benchmarks where Dalpha claimed the top position include Salesforce's GIFT-Eval, Deep Research Bench and Deep Research Bench II, a more rigorous evaluation framework. Dalpha achieved this performance with a proprietary general-purpose agent framework that delivered leading performance across multiple domains, rather than being optimized for a single task.

## Deep Research Agents

Deep Research (DR) agents are autonomous AI systems powered by Large Language Models that are designed to tackle complex, multi-turn informational research tasks by leveraging dynamic reasoning, adaptive long-horizon planning, multi-hop information retrieval, iterative tool use, and the generation of structured analytical reports.

A Deep Research Agent is an AI system with autonomous planning, multi-step reasoning, and tool usage abilities, capable of completing complex research tasks like a human researcher. Key capabilities include autonomy, persistence in long-cycle task execution, tool integration for real-time information, and structured output with citations and traceable sources.

PROMPT: Please write a literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market. Focus on how AI, as a key driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, is causing significant disruptions and affecting various industries. Ensure the review only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles.
====
# Literature Review: The Restructuring Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Labor Market

## Executive Summary

Artificial intelligence (AI), as a core technology of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, is restructuring labor markets less through immediate economy-wide job destruction than through a reallocation of tasks, skills, authority, and employment across occupations, firms, industries, and regions. The strongest journal evidence shows that AI and related automation technologies operate through several mechanisms: substituting for routine or codifiable tasks, augmenting workers in prediction- and knowledge-intensive tasks, changing employer demand for skills, increasing productivity in selected work settings, enabling algorithmic control, and widening differences between firms and workers that can use AI effectively and those that cannot.

The literature supports three balanced conclusions. First, displacement is real but uneven. Industrial robot evidence shows employment and wage losses in exposed U.S. local labor markets, especially in manufacturing, although cross-country and German evidence finds productivity gains and partial employment offsets [7–9]. Second, AI often restructures rather than eliminates jobs: vacancy, exposure, and firm-level studies show changes in AI-skill demand, occupational exposure, and firm growth without yet proving large aggregate employment declines [14–17, 30, 33–35]. Third, generative AI marks a new phase because it reaches writing, coding, consulting, customer support, and other cognitive tasks; journal evidence already shows large task-level productivity effects, but long-run wage and employment effects remain uncertain [18–24].

Overall, AI is best understood as a general-purpose restructuring force: it changes what workers do, which skills are valuable, how firms organize work, how managers control labor, and which industries experience disruption first.

---

## 1. Introduction: AI and the Fourth Industrial Revolution

The Fourth Industrial Revolution is commonly associated with the fusion of digital, physical, and biological technologies. In labor-market terms, AI is one of its most disruptive components because it extends automation beyond mechanical repetition into prediction, classification, language generation, recommendation, monitoring, and decision support. Unlike earlier waves of mechanization that primarily affected manual production, AI affects both blue-collar and white-collar work: factory production, logistics, customer support, software development, professional services, finance, healthcare, retail, platform work, and creative labor.

A central insight from the literature is that AI does not act on “jobs” as indivisible units. It acts on tasks. Jobs are bundles of tasks, some of which can be automated, some augmented, and some made more valuable because they remain human-intensive. This task-based view explains why AI can simultaneously reduce labor demand in one setting, raise productivity in another, and create new work elsewhere [1, 4–6].

This review therefore treats labor-market restructuring broadly. It includes changes in employment, wages, occupational composition, task content, skill demand, productivity, job quality, worker autonomy, surveillance, firm organization, and industry structure. It cites only English-language journal articles and distinguishes direct AI evidence from broader automation and robotics evidence.

---

## 2. Conceptual Foundations: Tasks, Automation, and Labor Demand

The foundational task-based literature begins with the argument that computerization substitutes for routine tasks while complementing nonroutine analytic and interactive work. Autor, Levy, and Murnane show that technological change is best understood through the changing task content of jobs rather than through a simple skilled-versus-unskilled distinction [1]. This framework explains why middle-skill routine clerical and production jobs became vulnerable to automation, while high-skill abstract work and low-skill in-person service work were more resilient.

Subsequent evidence on job polarization reinforces this view. In the United States, Autor and Dorn link the growth of low-skill service jobs and the decline of routine middle-skill employment to routine-task automation [2]. Goos, Manning, and Salomons find similar polarization across European labor markets, while also emphasizing that offshoring interacts with technology [3]. These studies are not direct evidence on modern generative AI, but they provide the core logic for understanding AI as a task-restructuring technology.

A second conceptual contribution is the distinction between automation and augmentation. Autor argues that machines often substitute for specific tasks while complementing human judgment, flexibility, interpersonal interaction, and problem solving [4]. Acemoglu and Restrepo formalize this distinction by separating three effects: the **displacement effect**, in which automation shifts tasks from labor to capital; the **productivity effect**, in which lower costs and higher output can raise demand for remaining labor tasks; and the **reinstatement effect**, in which new labor-intensive tasks emerge [5, 6]. This framework is crucial because it prevents the literature from collapsing into either technological pessimism or optimism.

For AI, the implication is clear: the labor-market impact depends on whether AI mainly automates existing tasks, complements workers in remaining tasks, or creates new tasks that require human oversight, judgment, integration, ethics, relationship management, or domain expertise.

---

## 3. From Computerization and Robots to AI

The most mature empirical evidence on automation-related disruption comes from industrial robotics. Robots are not equivalent to AI, but they provide a strong empirical bridge because they show how task substitution, productivity gains, and labor-market adjustment interact.

content.content:
PROMPT: Which Obsidian plugins can effectively replicate Notion's multi-view database functionality (including Table, Kanban, Calendar, and List views)? Please provide a detailed comparison of the strengths and weaknesses of these plugins.
============================================================
# Obsidian plugins that best replicate Notion-style multi-view databases

## Executive summary

If “replicate Notion databases” means **multiple saved views over the same underlying records**—Table, Kanban/Board, Calendar, and List, with filters, sorts, grouping, properties, and editable records—then the strongest Obsidian options are:

1. **Make.md** — best single-plugin, broad “Notion-like workspace/database” option. It documents list, table, calendar, and kanban/board context views over notes, plus properties and relational features. Its biggest weakness is complexity and less-certain view-by-view inline writeback documentation.  
2. **Obsidian Bases + Kanban/Board plugin + Calendar Bases** — best local-first, Obsidian-native stack. Native Bases gives Table/List; community Bases extensions add Kanban and Calendar. Strong for Markdown/YAML portability, weaker for all-in-one polish and maturity.  
3. **Database Views** by ekmungi — very Notion-like for Table, Kanban, Calendar, and Timeline/Gantt, with YAML/frontmatter editing and schema-like behavior, but **no distinct List view is documented**, and the plugin is newer/lower-adoption.  
4. **TaskNotes** — best if your “database” is mostly tasks. It gives task list, kanban, calendar/agenda, and Bases integration, but it is task-specific rather than a general-purpose Notion database replacement.  
5. **Note Database** — promising for Table, Board, Gallery, and List with inline editing and formulas, but **Calendar view is not documented**, so it is not a full four-view replacement.

Plugins such as **Dataview, Metadata Menu, Kanban, Full Calendar, and Tasks** are valuable building blocks, but they are better understood as a composable workflow stack, not a single unified Notion database replacement. **DB Folder** and **Obsidian Projects** are historically important but should be treated as high-risk for new setups because they are archived/removed/discontinued.

---

## What “Notion-like multi-view database” means

Notion databases are collections of page-backed records with structured properties and multiple views over the same data. Notion officially supports Table, Board/Kanban, Calendar, List, filters, sorts, grouping, and persistent view settings [1][2][3][4][5]. A true replacement should therefore do more than display a table: it should let the same records appear in multiple layouts, preserve filters/sorts/views, and ideally write changes back to the underlying record.

Obsidian’s model is different. Obsidian works with local Markdown files and YAML/frontmatter properties. Native **Bases** can create database-like views over files and properties, with official Table, Cards, List, and Map views, filters, sorts, formulas, grouping, and editable file properties [6][7][8][9][10][11]. But native Bases does **not** officially include Kanban or Calendar views; those require community plugins.

---

## Quick comparison matrix

| Option | Table | Kanban / Board | Calendar | List | Best for | Main weakness |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| **Make.md** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Broad Notion-like workspace/database replacement | Complex; exact inline writeback behavior is not equally documented for every view |
| **Obsidian Bases + Kanban Bases View/Base Board + Calendar Bases** | Yes | Yes, via plugin | Yes, via plugin | Yes | Local Markdown-first database dashboards | Multi-plugin stack; newer Bases extension ecosystem |
| **Database Views** by ekmungi | Yes | Yes | Yes | No documented distinct List | Notion-like folder/database mechanics | No List view; newer/lower-adoption; naming/install ambiguity |
| **TaskNotes** | Table-like via Bases/templates; task list native | Yes | Yes | Yes / task list | Task databases and personal task systems | Task-specific, not arbitrary databases |
| **Note Database** | Yes | Yes | No documented Calendar | Yes | Note databases with inline editing | Missing Calendar view |
| **Project Manager / Obsidian-PM** | Yes | Yes | No separate Calendar verified; Gantt instead | Not as general list database | Project execution | Project-specific; not a general Notion database |
| **Operon** | No verified first-class Table | Yes | Yes | Yes / task lists | Hybrid task management | Task-specific; Table view not verified |
| **Dataview + Metadata Menu + Kanban + Full Calendar** | Yes via Dataview/table queries | Yes via Kanban | Yes via Full Calendar | Yes via Dataview/Tasks | DIY power-user workflows | No unified database engine or consistent cross-view editing |
| **DB Folder** | Yes | Not verified | Not verified | Not verified | Legacy editable table databases | Archived/removed; high-risk |
| **Obsidian Projects** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No verified List | Historical project databases | Discontinued, archived, removed |

---

# 1. Make.md

**Best single-plugin candidate for broad Notion-like use.**

Make.md is the closest all-in-one answer if you want one plugin that feels broadly like a Notion workspace. Its documentation describes transforming notes into structured databases and visualizing them as **table, calendar, list, or kanban board** views [12][13]. The Obsidian plugin listing also describes organization with “lists, tables, calendars and boards” [15].

### Strengths

- **Covers all four requested views.** Make.md explicitly documents table, calendar, list, and kanban/board-style context views [12][13].
- **Works over notes and properties.** For Markdown files, Make.md stores properties directly in file frontmatter, preserving a local Markdown-backed model [14].
- **More than views.** It supports a broader workspace layer: contexts, relational trackers, formulas, rollups, and two-way relations are described in its project/plugin materials [12][15].
- **Good adoption signal.** The plugin is community-listed and has substantial usage compared with many newer database plugins [15].
- **Closest “Notion-like” feel.** It tries to change the broader Obsidian experience, not just add a table.

### Weaknesses

- **Complexity and UI overhead.** Make.md is not a small utility; it changes a lot of the Obsidian workflow. That can be powerful, but it can also feel heavy if you only want a database view.
- **Writeback evidence is not equally clear for every view.** Make.md documents editable context views and frontmatter-backed properties, and calendar drag/drop editing is documented, but primary docs do not clearly prove Notion-style inline property editing separately for every view [13][14].
- **Potential stability concerns.** Community reports have raised concerns about bugs or rough edges, so heavy users should test it with their own vault before committing.
- **Possible lock-in through plugin concepts.** Your notes remain Markdown, but Make.md’s contexts/workspace model adds plugin-specific structure.

### Verdict

**Best overall single-plugin option** if you want a Notion-like Obsidian environment with Table, Kanban, Calendar, and List views. Choose it if you value convenience and breadth over minimalism.

---

# 2. Obsidian Bases + Kanban/Board + Calendar Bases

**Best Obsidian-native, local-first stack.**

Native **Obsidian Bases** provides database-like views over Markdown files and properties. Officially, Bases supports Table, Cards, List, and Map views, plus filters, sorts, formulas, grouping, and property editing [6][7][8][9][10]. To get Notion’s Board/Kanban and Calendar equivalents, you add community Bases view plugins:

- **Base Board** or **Kanban Bases View** for Kanban/board views [16][17][18][19].
- **Calendar Bases** for calendar views over date properties [20][21].

### Strengths

- **Strong Markdown portability.** Records are ordinary Markdown files; properties live in YAML/frontmatter [6][11].
- **Native Table and List.** Bases officially supports Table and List views [7][9][10].
- **Kanban writeback is documented.** Base Board and Kanban Bases View both document property/frontmatter updates when cards move between columns [16][17][18][19].
- **Calendar writeback is documented.** Calendar Bases displays notes by date property and documents drag/drop rescheduling that updates note frontmatter [20][21].
- **Good future direction.** Bases is official Obsidian infrastructure, so it is likely to become the foundation for many future database-style workflows.
- **Composable.** You can choose the board/calendar plugins you prefer rather than adopting a large all-in-one system.

### Weaknesses

- **Not a single plugin.** You need multiple pieces: Bases plus a Kanban extension plus a Calendar extension.
- **Board and Calendar are not native Bases views.** They depend on community plugins and the stability of the Bases extension ecosystem.
- **Not full Notion parity.** Bases does not provide Notion-style relations, rollups, permissions, collaborative database behavior, or first-class cloud database semantics [6][7].
- **Data hygiene burden.** Since everything depends on consistent frontmatter fields, inconsistent property names or date formats can break views.
- **Maturity risk.** Calendar Bases and Bases board extensions are newer than long-established plugins.

### Verdict

**Best choice for users who want a clean, local-first, Markdown-native Notion-like setup.** It is less polished than Notion or Make.md, but it is more transparent and future-aligned with Obsidian’s own database direction.

---

# 3. Database Views by ekmungi

**Most Notion-like mechanics, but missing a documented List view.**

The current community plugin is **Database Views**, with plugin id/slug `database-views`, while the GitHub repository is `ekmungi/obsidian-database-plugin` [23][24][25]. Its docs/listing describe treating Markdown folders as databases with **Table, Kanban, Calendar, and Timeline/Gantt** views [23][25].

### Strengths

- **Strong database metaphor.** It treats a folder of Markdown files as a database and uses schema/view files to define columns and views [25].
- **Excellent three-view coverage.** Table, Kanban, and Calendar are documented, along with Timeline/Gantt [23][25].
- **Good writeback evidence.** It documents inline YAML/frontmatter editing and writing changes directly back to Markdown files [23][25].
- **Notion-like advanced behavior.** Evidence supports typed columns, per-view state, filters/sorts/grouping, wikilink relations, rollup-like computed columns, and templates [25].
- **Better “database” semantics than many visual plugins.** It is not merely a board or calendar; it is explicitly trying to create database views over Markdown records.

### Weaknesses

- **No distinct List view documented.** This prevents it from fully matching the user’s requested Table + Kanban + Calendar + List set.
- **Newer/lower-adoption risk.** It appears less battle-tested than Make.md, Dataview, Tasks, or native Bases.
- **Naming ambiguity.** The community plugin is “Database Views” with id `database-views`, but the repository name and some manual-install references use “obsidian-database-plugin,” which can confuse installation and documentation [23][24][25].
- **Potential schema/config lock-in.** It uses plugin-specific database/view configuration files.

### Verdict

**Excellent if you need Table + Kanban + Calendar database behavior and can live without a first-class List view.** It may be more database-like than Make.md in some mechanics, but it is less mature and less complete for the exact requested view set.

---

# 4. TaskNotes

**Best Notion-like database replacement for tasks.**

TaskNotes represents tasks as Markdown notes with frontmatter, then provides task-oriented views including lists, Kanban, calendars, agendas, and Bases templates [26][27][28][29][30].

### Strengths

- **Strong task database model.** Each task is a note, which makes it closer to a database row than a plain Markdown checkbox line [26].
- **Kanban and Calendar support.** TaskNotes documents Kanban views and calendar views, including drag/drop behavior for task status and scheduling [28][29].
- **Works with Bases.** It provides default Bases templates, which helps produce table-like views and dashboards [27].
- **Better for tasks than generic note databases.** Due dates, scheduled dates, status, recurrence, and agenda workflows are first-class concerns [26][29].
- **Markdown-first.** Task records remain local notes.

### Weaknesses

- **Task-specific.** It is not intended to be a general CRM, inventory, research library, or content database.
- **Table support is more indirect.** Table-like behavior is available through Bases/templates, but TaskNotes itself is framed around task lists, Kanban, calendar, and agenda views [27].
- **Schema is optimized for tasks.** For arbitrary databases, the task fields may feel constraining.

### Verdict

**The best choice if your Notion databases are mainly task/project trackers.** Not the best general-purpose replacement.

---

# 5. Note Database

**Good table/board/list database plugin, but not a full four-view replacement.**

Note Database turns Markdown files and frontmatter into editable, filterable, groupable databases. Its listing documents **Table, Board, Gallery, and List** views, along with formulas, filters, and inline editing [22].

Kanban movement, and Bases-compatible views.

Use **Tasks** if you prefer lightweight Markdown checkbox tasks and query-based lists, but do not expect full database-view parity.

### Best for project management

Use **Project Manager / Obsidian-PM** if you need Table + Kanban + Gantt project planning.

Use **TaskNotes** if you prefer task-note records and calendar/agenda workflows.

### Best for DIY power users

Use **Dataview + Metadata Menu + Kanban + Full Calendar + Tasks** if you want maximum flexibility and are comfortable maintaining conventions yourself.

### Plugins to avoid for new Notion-style database setups

Avoid **DB Folder** and **Obsidian Projects** for new setups because of archived/removed/discontinued status [46][47][48].

---

## Bottom line

For most users, the practical shortlist is:

1. **Make.md** — best single-plugin Notion-like database/workspace replacement.  
2. **Obsidian Bases + Kanban Bases View/Base Board + Calendar Bases** — best local-first, Obsidian-native, Markdown-backed database stack.  
3. **Database Views** — strong Table/Kanban/Calendar database mechanics, but lacks a documented List view.  
4. **TaskNotes** — best if your databases are mainly tasks.  
5. **Note Database** — good Table/Board/List option, but missing Calendar.

No Obsidian plugin perfectly duplicates Notion’s database system. The best choice depends on whether you value **all-in-one polish** (Make.md), **Markdown portability and official Obsidian direction** (Bases stack), **database mechanics** (Database Views), or **task-specific workflows** (TaskNotes).

content.content:
# 国内地铁/城轨碳滑板年用量、供应商份额与趋势判断

## 结论摘要

1. **没有公开的全国官方统计能直接回答“地铁碳滑板一年用多少根”。** 可用的做法是用车辆保有量、接触网受流比例、受电弓配置、滑板寿命和采购公告样本进行估算。
2. 按**中国内地、以地铁/城轨受电弓碳滑板为主口径**，2024—2025年前后的年需求量可估为：  
   **约3万—6万根/年，中位判断约4万根/年左右。**  
   其中，**存量运营替换是绝对主体**，新车初装约占小头，粗略为**2,000—4,000根/年**。
3. 若把第三轨/接触轨线路的**集电靴、受流器滑板**也放进“城市轨道交通车辆用炭滑板”广义口径，需求会增加，但这已不是狭义“受电弓碳滑板”，公开数据不足以可靠合并。
4. **供应商真实全国份额无法从公开资料可靠计算。** 公开招投标样本显示，北京万高众业、苏州东南佳、石家庄中车轨道交通装备、摩根系、新成新材等是高频或重要供应主体；但中标主体可能是制造商、代理商、车辆/备件集成商，不能直接等同制造份额。
5. 行业趋势是：**新增车辆配套放缓，存量维保替换占比上升；国产供应商进入主流线路，但外资/外资本土化品牌仍有存量和技术影响；材料、ADD、在线磨耗检测将提高产品门槛。**

---

## 1. 口径：这里说的“碳滑板”是什么

本报告主口径为：

> **中国内地城市轨道交通，主要是地铁车辆，在架空/刚性接触网受流系统中使用的受电弓碳滑板。**

需要和两个相近口径区分：

| 口径 | 是否纳入本报告主估算 | 说明 |
|---|---:|---|
| 受电弓碳滑板、受电弓滑板、弓条、滑板条 | 纳入 | 车顶受电弓与接触网接触取流的耗材 |
| 刚性接触网/柔性接触网线路 | 纳入 | 仍是受电弓—接触网系统 |
| 第三轨/接触轨集电靴滑板、受流器滑板 | 不纳入主估算，单列为广义口径 | 属于接触轨—集电靴系统，产品结构和数量逻辑不同 |
| 单轨、磁浮、APM、云巴等非典型地铁受电弓系统 | 原则上剔除或单列 | 不能按普通地铁受电弓碳滑板估算 |

交通运输部供电系统规范区分了接触网、接触轨、受电弓、集电靴等受流界面，这支持把“受电弓碳滑板”和“第三轨集电靴滑板”分开估算[4]。GB/T 38955—2020《城市轨道交通车辆用炭滑板》则提供了更广义的“城轨车辆用炭滑板”产品定义[5]。

---

## 2. 年用量估算：约3万—6万根/年

### 2.1 行业分母

中国城市轨道交通协会数据显示，截至2024年底，中国大陆城轨运营里程为12,160.77公里，其中地铁9,306.09公里；全城轨配属列车12,314列，其中地铁表列10,895列；全年运营车公里76.93亿车公里[1]。交通运输部2024年统计公报也显示，城轨和地铁规模已超过1万公里、配属车辆7.23万辆[2]。

但这些车辆并不全都使用受电弓碳滑板。北京、天津、武汉、青岛等城市存在较多第三轨/接触轨线路；部分城市还有单轨、有轨电车、磁浮、APM等制式。因此不能把10,895列地铁车辆全量作为受电弓碳滑板分母。

结合重点城市供电制式，较合理的估算是假设：

> **全国地铁/城轨中，需要按受电弓碳滑板计量的有效列车分母约8,000—9,500列。**

这是估算口径，不是官方统计。

### 2.2 单列车年用量参数

公开资料支持的核心参数如下：

| 参数 | 估算取值 | 依据 |
|---|---:|---|
| 每列受电弓数量 | 常用中位假设2台/列 | 天津A型6编组招标文件显示Mp车为带受电弓动车，可推导该项目为2台受电弓/列[6] |
| 每台受电弓滑板数量 | 2根/台 | 地铁智慧运维资料提到“同一受电弓两个碳滑板”并设置厚度差报警[7] |
| 单列装车滑板基数 | 约4根/列 | 2台受电弓 × 2根/台 |
| 年走行强度 | 约8万—12万公里/列年 | 由全国运营车公里和列车保有量推算，城市差异较大 |
| 单根寿命 | 约8万—15万公里，中高磨耗情形更低 | 重庆地铁样本推导约15万公里量级；智慧运维资料使用厚度、磨耗率和剩余寿命监测，而非固定周期[7][8] |
| 异常磨耗/库存系数 | 约1.15—1.35 | 反映弓网异常、库存补采、提前更换和采购口径差异 |

因此：

> **单列车年需求 ≈ 3—6根/列年。**

这个值与公开采购样本存在差异。比如石家庄2025年度2、3号线项目披露860根、合同金额144.48万元，折算约1,680元/根，但该类年度采购可能包含库存补采、跨期采购或专项备件，不能直接等同实际年消耗[10][11]。

### 2.3 全国年用量区间

用公式估算：

> **年度需求 = 接触网受电列车数 × 单列年需求**

| 情景 | 有效列车分母 | 单列年需求 | 存量替换需求 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| 低情景 | 8,000列 | 3根/列年 | 约2.4万根 |
| 中情景 | 8,500—9,000列 | 4—5根/列年 | 约3.4万—4.5万根 |
| 高情景 | 9,500列 | 5—6根/列年 | 约4.8万—5.7万根 |

再加上新车初装。2024年全国城轨车辆采购3,784辆，其中地铁车辆2,840辆；车辆交付5,473辆[3]。若按6辆编组、每列约4根初装滑板估算，新车初装需求大致为：

> **约2,000—4,000根/年。**

所以主判断为：

| 口径 | 年用量估算 |
|---|---:|
| 存量运营替换 | 约2.5万—5.5万根/年 |
| 新车初装配套 | 约0.2万—0.4万根/年 |
| 合计，受电弓碳滑板主口径 | **约3万—6万根/年** |
| 中位判断 | **约4万根/年左右** |

### 2.4 金额规模粗估

公开样本中，石家庄项目可弱校验单价约1,600—1,700元/根[10][11]。不同城市、型号、是否含托架/组件/ADD气道、运输税费和服务会造成较大差异。

若按**1,600—2,500元/根**估算：

> **国内地铁/城轨受电弓碳滑板年度市场金额约0.5亿—1.5亿元。**

这只是碳滑板本体和相近采购口径的粗估；若采购标的包含受电弓组件、ADD、检测服务、框架备件包，金额会显著高于单根滑板口径。

---

## 3. 供应商格局与份额

### 3.1 先说限制：公开份额不等于真实市场份额

目前公开资料不足以得出“全国真实市场份额”。原因有四个：

1. 很多碳滑板随新车、受电弓总成或备件包采购，不单独披露。
2. 年度采购公告常有金额无数量，或有数量无金额。
3. 中标主体可能是制造商，也可能是代理商、授权供货商、车辆/备件集成商。
4. 不同城市使用原厂件、试用准入件、单一来源件、框架备件，公开可见度差异很大。

因此，以下份额只能称为：

> **公开招投标样本中的合同主体金额份额。**

### 3.2 公开样本金额份额

基于已披露金额、项目名称明确为碳滑板/滑板条/组件的样本，主要可计算的是北京万高众业与石家庄中车轨道交通装备。

| 样本口径 | 北京万高众业 | 石家庄中车轨道交通装备 | 说明 |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| 样本A：已披露历史合同金额样本 | 约81.9% | 约18.1% | 样本总额约796.1万元；北京万高覆盖济南、成都、天津、石家庄、北京等合同线索；石家庄中车为石家庄项目[10][12] |
| 样本B：样本A + 石家庄2025—2026年3号线第一候选项目 | 约67.6% | 约32.4% | 将石家庄中车168万元第一候选报价计入后得到；仍非最终全国份额[10] |

这个样本过小，且石家庄中车是否为实际碳滑板制造商尚未穿透，因此不能把上述比例当作全国市场份额。

### 3.3 主要供应商定位

| 企业/品牌 | 角色判断 | 证据与备注 |
|---|---|---|
| **北京万高众业科技股份有限公司** | 国内重要碳滑板制造商/直接供货商 | 公开样本金额最高；永贵电器公告称其主要经营轨道交通受电弓碳滑板等业务[18]；CRCC公告出现其碳滑板认证信息[17] |
| **苏州东南佳新材料股份有限公司** | 国内重要制造商 | 上海地铁单一来源公示明确其为碳滑板产品生产厂商[15]；公开转让说明书披露其主营轨道交通受电弓碳滑板、滑板碳条研发生产销售[19] |
| **石家庄中车轨道交通装备有限公司** | 公开样本中重要合同主体；制造商属性需穿透 | 石家庄项目中标/候选金额较高[10]；但公告允许制造商或授权代理商投标，不能直接判定其为碳滑板实际制造商 |
| **大同新成新材料/新成新材** | 国内制造商/材料商，城轨份额待核 | 年报披露受电弓碳滑板、CRCC、天津地铁等考核线索[20]；但本轮未核到高质量城轨中标金额样本 |
| **摩根系 Morgan/摩根新材料上海** | 外资/外资本土化重要品牌 | 摩根上海工厂资料显示其生产轨道交通碳滑板、集电靴等[22]；在南京、郑州等采购线索中出现 |
| **Mersen 美尔森、Schunk** | 国际受流材料/系统供应商 | 美尔森产品覆盖受电弓碳滑板等轨交受流件[23]；Schunk更多还涉及受电弓系统和碳材料，需区分总成商与滑板制造商 |
| **宜兴溢洋墨根、辽宁红德、邢台瑞普等** | 技术/认证/标准或候选供应商线索 | 在标准、认证、专利或行业资料中出现；城轨地铁公开中标份额需进一步核验 |

### 3.4 竞争格局的真实含义

目前更合理的判断不是“某一家占全国多少”，而是：

- **北京万高众业、苏州东南佳**是公开证据最强的国内碳滑板制造商。
- **摩根、Mersen、Schunk**等外资或外资本土化品牌仍在部分线路、原装件、碳条材料或高端产品中有影响。
- **中车系主体**可能在车辆、受电弓总成、备件集成和授权供货中扮演重要角色，但不能整体合并为“碳滑板制造份额”。
- 运营商采购更像是**线路/车型白名单 + 试用验证 + 年度备件采购**，而不是完全标准化的大宗商品市场。福州地铁采购文件就体现了原装产品、试用评审通过品牌等准入要求[24]。

---

## 4. 行业趋势

### 4.1 从新增建设驱动转向存量维保驱动

2024年城轨运营里程、配属车辆和运营车公里继续增长，但车辆采购数量和车辆购置投资下降。协会装备统计显示，2024年城轨车辆采购3,784辆，同比下降30.05%；交付5,473辆，同比下降10.04%[3]。

这意味着：

- 新车初装碳滑板需求增长放缓；
- 存量车辆保有量扩大，替换需求更稳定；
- 年度采购、框架备件、检修备件的重要性上升。

对碳滑板企业来说，行业重心会从“跟随新线和新车放量”转向“进入更多运营线路的维保体系”。

### 4.2 用量和运营强度更相关

碳滑板是磨耗件，核心驱动不是线路里程本身，而是：

- 列车走行公里；
- 开行列次；
- 弓网接触质量；
- 接触网硬点、拉弧、偏磨；
- 运营商检修阈值和库存策略。

智慧运维资料已经用剩余厚度、磨耗率、同弓两滑板厚度差等指标管理碳滑板状态[7]。因此未来需求会更依赖运营强度和状态修数据，而不是简单按新增线路估算。

### 4.3 国产化继续推进，但不是简单替代外资

国内供应商已经进入多地采购和候选名单，北京万高众业、苏州东南佳、新成新材等具备较强国产化基础[17][19][20]。但外资品牌仍有三类影响：

1. 存量线路原装件；
2. 碳条材料和材料技术；
3. 外资本土化工厂，例如摩根上海[22]。

所以更准确的趋势是：

> **国产品牌份额提升，但核心材料、历史准入和外资本土工厂仍会共同存在。**

### 4.4 材料升级：浸金属碳、复合炭、抗电弧和抗冲击

GB/T 38955—2020将城市轨道交通车辆用炭滑板材料分为纯炭、浸金属炭、复合炭等类别[5]。材料研究表明，浸金属碳可提升导电性和强度，但仍面临电弧烧蚀、裂纹、掉块和界面结合问题[25]。

趋势方向包括：

- 更高导电性；
- 更低磨耗率；
- 更好抗冲击、抗掉块；
- 更稳定的粘接和气密结构；
- 与ADD自动降弓和在线监测系统兼容。

这会提高供应商的材料、加工、检测和线路试用门槛。

### 4.5 智能检测会改变更换策略

在线检测、激光轮廓测量、图像识别和磨耗趋势预测会推动检修从定期更换向状态修转变。短期看，检测更严格可能发现更多隐患，增加替换；长期看，状态修有可能减少过早更换，提高单根滑板利用率。

因此，智能检测对行业的影响不是简单“减少用量”，而是：

- 提高高质量产品的准入价值；
- 降低异常事故；
- 推动运营商更关注寿命曲线和全生命周期成本；
- 使供应商从单纯卖耗材，转向耗材 + 检测 + 寿命管理。

### 4.6 供电制式决定产品结构

第三轨/接触轨线路不应纳入受电弓碳滑板主口径。北京、天津

## DeepResearch Bench Overview

DeepResearch Bench is a benchmark consisting of 100 PhD-level research tasks, each meticulously crafted by domain experts across 22 distinct fields. The benchmark includes 50 tasks in Chinese and 50 in English.

## Evaluation Frameworks

The benchmark proposes RACE and FACT, two novel evaluation frameworks that respectively assess the report generation quality and the information retrieval abilities of Deep Research Agents. More specifically:

- RACE is a reference-based method with adaptive criteria to assess the quality of generated research reports.

# Predictive and scheduled Kubernetes node autoscaling: effective strategies and projects

## Executive summary

For Kubernetes node counts, there is no single portable “predictive Cluster Autoscaler” that is mature, Kubernetes-native, provider-neutral, and production-standard. The practical answer is to combine **one node-capacity owner** with one or more proactive demand-shaping mechanisms:

1. **Known recurring peaks:** use **scheduled scaling** of node-pool minimums, provider ASG/VMSS/MIG/node-pool bounds, Karpenter static `NodePool.spec.replicas`, or scheduled workload replicas.
2. **Business-metric or event-driven demand:** use **KEDA/HPA external metrics** to scale workloads early, then let **Karpenter or Cluster Autoscaler** provision nodes when those pods become unschedulable.
3. **Latency-sensitive spikes:** use **overprovisioning / placeholder pods** or warm static node pools so real pods can start on already-running nodes.
4. **Non-elastic or governed node groups:** build a **custom controller/operator** or use **Cluster API MachineDeployment/MachinePool** to reconcile desired capacity from schedules, forecasts, approvals, or GitOps.
5. **AWS-specific option:** EC2 Auto Scaling **scheduled scaling** and **predictive scaling** can act on ASGs that back EKS node groups, but this is ASG-level, not Kubernetes-native, and must be coordinated carefully with Cluster Autosc

input.architecture:
PUBLISHED RECORD: essentially nothing. Searched arXiv, GitHub, HF, company site, and Korean-language press (달파, 딥리서치, 에이전트). There is NO paper, NO code, NO tech report, NO model/dataset card for dalpha-deepresearch. What exists is press: (a) TheElec (thelec.net/news/articleView.html?idxno=11532) — Dalpha claims #1 on GIFT-Eval, DeepResearch Bench and DRB-II with "a proprietary general-purpose agent framework" not task-specialized; no scores, no architecture, no third-party verification. (b) ETNews 2026-05-07 (etnews.com/20260507000224) — framework named "Cobra"; scored 79.11% on OpenAI MLE-Bench (vs Google CAIR MARS+ 62.67, Baidu FM-Agent 64.44); described as using a "world model methodology" that internally simulates environment-model interaction to predict future outcomes, enabling autonomous exploration and self-improvement; company says a paper is PLANNED but not published. Treat "world model" as marketing until a paper lands. So the architecture below is reverse-engineered from the 100 artifacts, not from any disclosure.

RETRIEVAL (inferred): deliberately SMALL and primary-source-biased. Median 30 unique URLs per report (range 7-92) vs gemini-2.5-pro 106, bodhi 60, openai-DR 41. Sources are official docs, GitHub repos, standards (GB/T), ministry statistics, journal DOIs — not blogspam. On the lit-review task (id 72) every one of 35 refs is a journal article with a DOI and zero web URLs; on the Obsidian task (id 66) sources are obsidian.md/help pages, plugin listings and repo manifest.json files. So retrieval is TARGETED AT AUTHORITATIVE ARTIFACTS and the source TYPE is selected by task genre (a genre-conditioned retrieval policy). Median 69 inline [n] markers over ~30 sources = each source is re-cited ~2.3x; citation density 15 markers/1k words, ~1 per 67 words.

OUTLINE: bespoke, not templated. Heading uniqueness across all 100 reports = 0.896 (2993 unique of 3339 headings). The only invariants are: H1 title (100%), an executive-summary-type opening section (78/100: "Executive summary"/"执行摘要"/"核心结论"/"结论摘要"/"Short answer"/"Bottom line"), a Conclusion (~65/100), and "## Sources" as the last section (100/100). Everything between is derived from the question. It writes ANSWER-SHAPED outlines, not TOPIC-SHAPED ones (e.g. id 68 K8s: "Why standard Cluster Autoscaler may not be enough" -> "Strategy taxonomy" -> "Patterns A-F" -> "Provider-specific" -> "Recommended architectures IF demand is calendar-predictable / IF event-driven / IF latency-critical").

LENGTH: the shortest report on the board by 2-4x. Median 5,224 words (EN 3,956 / CN 6,290) vs cellcog-max 20,957, gemini 16,144, lunon 14,998, WhaleCloud 10,296, bodhi 9,883. It beats gemini (49.98) by 3.1 pts with 1/3 the words and 1/4 the sources. corr(words, insight) within dalpha = 0.21 — length is nearly irrelevant to its own scores.

MICRO-FORM: median paragraph 37 words (short, one-claim paragraphs). Bullets are SHORT enumerations (median 9 words; only 20% bold-led) — unlike gemini (56-word bullets = bullet-as-paragraph) or cellcog (23 words, 55% bold-led). Argument lives in prose and tables; bullets only enumerate. 83/100 use tables, 91/100 use --- rules between sections. Reads as high-signal-per-word, which is why readability = 53.20, the HIGHEST of any system on the board.
input.composition_method:
THE KEY MECHANISM — "the inference lives in the SCHEMA, not in the sentences."

dalpha does NOT get insight by writing speculative sentences. Almost every declarative sentence is still tightly attached to a citation (15 markers/1k words). What it does instead is INVENT AN ORGANIZING FRAME that no source contains, and then populate that frame with cited facts. The novel/analytic content is carried by structure, not by unverifiable prose. Four concrete frame types, all observed:

1. SCOPE CONTRACT (口径 / "What X means"). ~31/100 open with a definitional section that states what counts and what doesn't, usually as an inclusion/exclusion table. id 44 opens with a table of five scope variants and marks each "included in main estimate / excluded, reported separately". id 66 opens with "What 'Notion-like multi-view database' means" and derives 4 required capabilities BEFORE naming any candidate. This is a judgment (it is an authorial decision) that reads as rigor, and it makes every later claim commensurable. It is the single cheapest insight+instruction-following lever on the board.

2. TRANSPARENT ARITHMETIC MODEL (the Fermi engine). This is how it produces numbers that exist in NO source without hallucinating. id 44 (annual demand for metro pantograph carbon strips, insight 0.570): (a) fleet denominator from official statistics [1][2][3]; (b) a PARAMETER TABLE where every row is (parameter | estimated value | BASIS — with citation or "this is an estimation convention, not official statistics"); (c) a LOW/MID/HIGH SCENARIO TABLE producing 24k-57k units; (d) plus new-build fitment, giving "30k-60k/yr, median judgment ~40k/yr"; (e) an INDEPENDENT CROSS-CHECK against a real procurement tender (860 units / RMB 1.4448M -> RMB 1,680/unit) and an explicit reconciliation of why the check disagrees; (f) a monetary estimate = quantity x unit price. Every INPUT is span-verifiable. The COMBINATION step is explicitly labeled as authorial estimate. That is cross-source inference done in a form that survives a fact-checker.

3. EVIDENCE-STATUS LEDGER (turning non-findings into insight). 40/100 contain an explicit "the evidence cannot establish X" block, and crucially it is placed BEFORE the weak numbers, not buried. id 44 §3.1: "public tender share != real market share" with four enumerated reasons, then presents share tables labeled "Sample A" / "Sample B" with the sample total (RMB 7.961M) and refuses to generalize. id 66's comparison matrix contains cells reading "No documented distinct List view", "Not verified", "no separate Calendar verified" — and it then DERIVES a conclusion from the absence ("therefore not a full four-view replacement"). Absence of evidence is converted into an analytic distinction instead of an omission. This is why an extraction-grounded system does not have to lie to gain insight: "not documented" is itself a verifiable, load-bearing claim.

4. VERDICT + ROUTER LAYER (commitment). It commits to an answer. Per-item "### Verdict" blocks (id 66: every candidate gets Strengths / Weaknesses / Verdict), a global "## Bottom line" / "最终判断" / "结论先说" / "Short answer", and a CONDITIONAL ROUTER: 48/100 contain "Best for X -> use Y" / "If demand is calendar-predictable -> ..." / "如果你...". This is a decision function over the evidence, which no single source supplies. Note it wins on the RANKING itself: the id 66 exec summary is a ranked 1-5 shortlist with one-line reasons, produced before any evidence is shown.

PROCESS SHAPE (inferred, medium confidence): the invariant skeleton (title -> exec summary -> bespoke body -> conclusion -> Sources) plus near-total heading uniqueness plus the fact that the exec summary is a ranked/quantified ANSWER that presupposes the whole body implies a plan-then-write pipeline where the OUTLINE IS SYNTHESIZED FIRST FROM AN EVIDENCE LEDGER, then sections are written against it, then the exec summary is written LAST (or rewritten last) from the finished verdicts. Evidence for a late/global pass: exec summary numbers, the mid-report scenario table and the conclusion agree exactly in id 44; the id 66 exec summary ranking is identical to the Bottom-line ranking 4,000 words later. Evidence AGAINST a heavy multi-agent parallel-section writer: no section-boundary repetition, no redundant re-introductions, uniform voice, no per-section source silos (the same [n] indices recur across distant sections, so the bibliography is GLOBAL and fixed before writing, not per-section). I found NO direct evidence of an adversarial critic pass; the "not verified / not documented" language is better explained by a verification pass over CLAIMS (each cell/parameter checked against a source, with the check's outcome written into the text) than by a prose critic.

WHAT IT DOES NOT DO: it does not pad. It does not template. It does not bury argument in bullets. It does not maximize sources. It is last among the top-5 on comprehensiveness (52.58) and first on the whole board on readability (53.20) — it deliberately trades breadth for tightness and still ranks 5th.

GPT-5.5 board: overall 53.10 (rank 5). Dimension profile is the OPPOSITE of the rest of the top-5: comprehensiveness 52.58 (LOWEST of the top 5 — cellcog 56.34, WhaleCloud 55.14, bodhi 54.15, lunon 53.42), insight 52.94 (also lowest of top-5), instruction-following 53.87 (2nd on the whole board), readability 53.20 (HIGHEST ON THE ENTIRE BOARD — nobody else clears 52.5; cellcog 51.94, gemini 49.58).

So dalpha is not a "more of everything" system. It is the efficiency outlier: it is the shortest report on the board (5.2k words vs a 10-21k pack) with the second-fewest sources, and it converts that into the best readability and near-top instruction-following while sacrificing raw coverage. It out-scores the gemini reference by +3.12 overall and +3.02 insight using ~1/3 the words and ~1/4 the URLs.

Relative to POLARIS (43.82, insight 42.38): the gap to dalpha's insight is +10.6 points and it is NOT explained by length, source count, or coverage — dalpha has less of all three than several systems we already beat on those axes. The gap is explained entirely by the four frame mechanisms above (scope contract, arithmetic model, evidence-status ledger, verdict/router). This is the single best system on the board to copy, because it is the only top-5 system whose advantage does not require us to write 20k words.
what_we_can_steal: THE BIG ONE — move inference into the SCHEMA, not the sentences. dalpha keeps ~every declarative sentence citation-attached (15 markers/1k words) yet scores insight 52.94. The non-extractable content is carried by the FRAME: the axis of comparison, the taxonomy, the arithmetic, the verdict. We can keep span verification 100% intact and still gain insight, because the frame is authorial by construction and no verifier should be asked to span-check it. Our 'extraction-grounded' constraint is NOT the cause of our insight score; our lack of an authorial layer is.

SCOPE CONTRACT (口径 section). Open with a section that defines what counts and what does not, ideally as an inclusion/exclusion table (dalpha: 31/100, and it is the FIRST body section on the hardest tasks). It costs ~200 words, is fully verifiable, and it is simultaneously an insight signal (you made a defensible analytic choice) and an instruction-following signal (you addressed prompt ambiguity explicitly). Cheapest lever we have.

TRANSPARENT ARITHMETIC MODEL for any quantity the sources do not state. Pattern from id 44, copy it literally: (1) denominator from an official stat [cite]; (2) a PARAMETER TABLE with columns (parameter | value | BASIS) where every row is either cited or explicitly stamped 'estimation convention, not official statistics'; (3) a LOW/MID/HIGH scenario table; (4) a stated range plus a single committed median judgment; (5) an INDEPENDENT cross-check against a different source type and an explicit reconciliation of the discrepancy. Every input is span-verifiable; only the multiplication is authorial, and it is labeled. This is cross-source inference that a fact-checker cannot fault.

EVIDENCE-STATUS LEDGER — make our verification failures into content. Today a span we cannot verify becomes an omission (pure loss). dalpha writes 'No public official statistic can answer this directly', 'no distinct List view is documented', 'Not verified', 'public tender share != real market share, for four reasons', and then DERIVES conclusions from the absence. Our verifier already knows exactly which claims failed verification — we are throwing away the highest-insight-density signal we produce. Emit it: a comparison matrix whose cells carry evidence status, and a 'what the evidence cannot establish' block placed BEFORE the weak numbers.

COMMIT TO A VERDICT. Per-item '### Verdict' blocks and a global '## Bottom line' / '最终判断' / 'Short answer'. dalpha ranks its options in the executive summary BEFORE showing evidence, then re-states the identical ranking at the end. A ranking is a claim no source makes — and it is the highest-yield insight token per word on this benchmark.

CONDITIONAL ROUTER (48/100). Close with 'Best for X -> use Y' / 'If demand is calendar-predictable -> pattern A; if latency-critical -> pattern E'. It is a decision function over the evidence, cheap to generate from the comparison matrix, and it reads as expert judgment.

INVENT NAMED ABSTRACTIONS. id 68 coins 'Pattern A-F' and a 'strategy taxonomy' that appear in no source, then fills them with cited facts. Naming the axes IS the synthesis. Our pipeline currently only ever reuses the sources' own framing.

STOP PADDING. dalpha is the SHORTEST report on the board (5.2k median words, EN 3.9k) and ranks 5th, beating the 16k-word gemini reference on every dimension. If POLARIS is generating length to chase comprehensiveness, that is negative-value: comprehensiveness is dalpha's WORST dimension and it still ranks 5. Reasoning-per-word is what the RACE judge rewards.

SHORT PARAGRAPHS, SHORT BULLETS. Median paragraph 37 words, median bullet 9 words, bullets used only for enumeration and never as paragraph containers. This is the mechanical driver of dalpha's board-leading readability 53.20. gemini's 56-word bullets correlate with its 49.58 readability.

GENRE-CONDITIONED RETRIEVAL + CITATION FORMAT. On the 'cite only high-quality English journal articles' task (id 72) dalpha cites 35 DOIs and ZERO web URLs; on the software task it cites official docs, plugin listings and repo manifest.json. Source TYPE is selected from the prompt. Small, authoritative source set (median 30 URLs), each re-cited ~2.3x — not a 100-URL dump. Retrieval breadth is not the win condition.

GLOBAL BIBLIOGRAPHY, THEN WRITE. The same [n] indices recur across distant sections, so the reference list is fixed before composition (not per-section silos). Freeze the evidence ledger, synthesize the outline FROM the ledger, write sections against it, then write the executive summary LAST from the finished verdicts so the headline numbers/rankings match the body exactly (they do, in every report I checked).
